text: The surgeon works with a thin peritoneum to create a large, wide flap, crossing over the midline above the hernia. The lack of abdominal wall fat plane presents a challenge, but careful technique allows progress. The flap creation becomes easier as it approaches the midline.
The surgeon assesses the hernia sac, noting the difficulty in preserving the peritoneum due to its fusion with the sac. A large defect is created in the hernia sac during the procedure.

The surgeon dissects the peritoneum around the hernia sac, unable to remove it from the defect. The flap is extended laterally, similar to a transversus abdominis release (TAR) dissection, reaching towards the opposite side.

The surgeon dissects down the transversalis muscle towards the retroperitoneum, aiming to extend the flap across the midline to the ipsilateral side. The hernia defect is visible, measuring approximately four centimeters wide and three centimeters long.

The surgeon closes the hernia defect transversely, ensuring the closure extends past the ipsilateral flap creation. The flap is dissected laterally to cover the mesh completely, preventing exposure. The pressure is reduced to eight for a tension-free closure.

The surgeon closes the large hole in the peritoneal flap transversely, ensuring the flap extends across the ipsilateral side past the initial creation point. The closure is visually close to the start of the flap creation and comes together smoothly.

The surgeon measures the hernia defect, confirming it is suitable for the 15x15 mesh. A 3-0 silk suture is placed in the center of the mesh and tacked to the center of the hernia defect. Additional tacks are applied around the corners of the mesh to secure it in place.

The surgeon closes the flap, extending it past the lateral corner of the mesh on the ipsilateral side to ensure complete coverage. The flap successfully covers the mesh without tearing, despite the mesh not being entirely inside the peritoneal defect.
The surgeon inspects the closed defect and identifies a few small holes, which are individually closed using 3-0 Vicryl sutures. The final result of the hernia repair is visually satisfactory.
